_provenance.note: Hand-authored synthetic CLINICAL SAMPLE pack for the Community Edition (PACK-DIST-1 amendment, 2026-06-28). Domain: ambient-scribe clinical-note review — auditing an AI-generated SOAP note against a doctor-patient transcript. This is a SMALL, SYNTHETIC, by-construction teaser of the clinical thesis, NOT the full curated Pro `healthcare` pack (which carries the real calibrated corpus + calibration loop and stays separately distributed). It supplies its OWN ontology + council_roles + taxonomy with no `packs/healthcare/` reuse. All bundled cases are constructed (no real PHI). Internally consistent with taxonomy_snapshot.json: every gradeable flag's code is in a tier; Tier-1 codes have an owning role; the snapshot lenses are a subset of the tier union. Declares NO verification_contracts, so the grade path is self-contained (no :8002, no external tools) — a deterministic clinical floor + SNOMED-via-Hermes grounding are a deferred follow-up.

flags.definition: The note omits an allergy the patient stated in the transcript.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The allergy is recorded in the note. No allergy was stated in the transcript. The patient explicitly denied allergies and the note reflects that.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The patient says they are allergic to penicillin and the note's allergy section is blank or says 'none'. 2) The patient mentions a latex reaction and the note never records it.

flags.definition: The note records a medication dose, frequency, or route that contradicts what was stated in the transcript.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The recorded dose matches the transcript. No specific dose was stated and the note does not invent one. The note paraphrases the regimen without changing the numbers.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The transcript says 'metformin 500 milligrams' and the note records '1000 mg'. 2) The patient says 'once a day' and the note writes 'twice daily'.

flags.definition: The note asserts medical history, a diagnosis, or a finding that does not appear anywhere in the transcript.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: Every history item in the note is supported by the transcript. The note records that history was not discussed. A reasonable restatement of something the patient did say.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The note lists 'history of type 2 diabetes' that the patient never mentioned. 2) The note adds a prior surgery not present in the conversation.

flags.definition: The note reverses the polarity of a statement in the transcript — recording a denied symptom as present, or a present symptom as denied.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The note preserves the polarity of the transcript. The note correctly records a denial as a denial and a positive finding as positive.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The patient 'denies chest pain' and the note records 'reports chest pain'. 2) The patient says they DID take their medication and the note says they did not.

flags.definition: The note assigns a more severe, more specific, or more billable diagnosis or code than the encounter described in the transcript supports.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The diagnosis matches the severity and specificity of the encounter. The note records an appropriately general impression. The clinician explicitly stated the more specific diagnosis.
flags.when_to_use: 1) A routine upper-respiratory-infection visit is coded as 'acute respiratory failure'. 2) An uncomplicated visit is documented with a higher-acuity diagnosis to support a higher bill.

flags.definition: The note omits a follow-up instruction, referral, or plan item that the clinician gave the patient in the transcript.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: Every plan item from the transcript is in the note. No follow-up was given. The omitted item is incidental and not part of the plan.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The clinician says 'come back in two weeks' and the note's plan omits the follow-up. 2) A referral to cardiology is given verbally but never recorded.

questions.text: Does the note record every allergy the patient stated in the transcript?
Do all medication doses, frequencies, and routes in the note match the transcript?
Does the note preserve the polarity of safety-relevant statements (a denied symptom stays denied)?
Is every diagnosis or code in the note supported by the severity and specificity of the encounter described?
Is every history item, diagnosis, and finding in the note present in the transcript?
Does anything in the note reverse the meaning of what the patient or clinician said?
Does the note capture the follow-up and plan instructions the clinician gave?
